title: Advanced Materials and Structural Analysis
data_note: Materials data from peer-reviewed literature and AARO/ORNL analysis. Strong force parameters from QCD standard values.

statistics.context: Range depends on chirality, diameter, and defect density of individual nanotubes.
statistics.verification_note: Theoretical limit; individual tube measurements approach this range

source.name: Bai, Y. et al. Nature Nanotechnology 13, 589 (2018)

statistics.context: Achieved in centimeter-long CNT bundles. Highest experimental measurement as of 2026.
Among the stiffest known materials. Sets the theoretical tensile strength limit at ~E/10 ≈ 110 GPa.
statistics.verification_note: Widely reproduced across multiple independent measurements

source.name: Derived from tensile strength and density (~1300 kg/m³)

statistics.context: Approximately 300× greater than high-carbon steel. Key metric for aerospace structural applications.
statistics.verification_note: Calculated from measured tensile strength and known CNT density

statistics.context: Measured by AFM nanoindentation of suspended monolayer graphene membranes.
Stiffest known 2D material. Consistent with CNT measurements (same sp² carbon bonding).

statistics.metric: Theoretical ultimate tensile strength limit (any material)

source.name: Frenkel (1926) theoretical analysis of ideal crystal strength

statistics.context: For carbon-based materials (E ~ 1 TPa), this gives ~100 GPa, closely matching individual CNT measurements.
statistics.verification_note: Classical result in materials science; validated by CNT and graphene data

source.name: Industry and academic CNT fiber data

statistics.context: Gap between single-tube (~80 GPa) and bulk fiber (~few GPa) due to defects, weak inter-tube shear, processing.
statistics.verification_note: Widely reported in materials science literature

source.name: Zhang, R. et al. Nature Communications 10, 3848 (2019)

statistics.context: Defect concentration is the primary factor limiting bulk CNT material performance.

statistics.metric: Strong nuclear force coupling constant at nuclear scale
statistics.context: QCD coupling αs ≈ 1 at ~10⁻¹⁵ m scale. Compared to EM coupling α ≈ 1/137 ≈ 7.3 × 10⁻³.
Strong force bonds are ~100× stronger than electromagnetic bonds at relevant scales.
statistics.verification_note: One of the most precisely measured constants in physics

statistics.metric: Quark-gluon plasma existence duration in colliders

source.name: RHIC/BNL and LHC/CERN heavy-ion collision data

statistics.context: QGP produced in Au-Au and Pb-Pb collisions at RHIC and LHC. Too brief for materials application.
statistics.verification_note: Extensively documented in RHIC and LHC experimental programs
